attributes.stem:
A six-month-old girl presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of worsening rash around her anus. Her parent reports the rash is red, slightly raised, and appears uncomfortable. The child has been eating and drinking normally but seems irritable during nappy changes. She has dry erythematous patches on her flexures that she scratches regularly, and takes no regular medications. Her immunisations are up to date.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is bright erythema with raised edges in the nappy area, involving the inguinal folds. Several small red lesions are visible at the periphery of the main rash. The affected areas are warm to touch but not weeping or bleeding.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis for her presenting complaint?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation is classic for candidal dermatitis (nappy dermatitis, nappy rash), with bright erythema, raised edges, involving the skin folds, and characteristic satellite lesions. Candidal dermatitis typically involves the deep inguinal folds and has surrounding satellite lesions. Candida albicans is an opportunistic yeast that thrives in the warm, moist environment created by diapers. Nappy rash or diaper rash is the broad term to describe the irritant contact dermatitis affecting the area of skin enclosed by a nappy, and this can be complicated by candida, bacterial (streptococcal), threadworm or viral infections (molluscum, herpes simplex virus, enterovirus).

Irritant contact dermatitis is the most common form of diaper rash and typically affects the areas in direct contact with urine and faeces, typically sparing the inguinal folds. It presents as confluent erythema without satellite lesions and usually improves with barrier protection and frequent changes.

Seborrhoeic dermatitis in infants typically affects the scalp (cradle cap), face, and neck, though it can occur in the diaper area. It presents as salmon-colored patches with greasy yellow scale, without satellite lesions.

Atopic dermatitis rarely presents solely in the diaper area and is typically associated with dry, scaly patches in other areas such as the face, trunk, or extremities. It is intensely pruritic and often associated with a family history of atopy. It is likely that she has atopic dermatitis concurrently, but it is not the cause of the rash around her bottom.

Psoriasis in the diaper area presents as well-demarcated, bright red plaques with minimal scale due to the moisture of the area. It often appears in other areas such as the scalp or extremities and may be associated with nail changes.

Management of candidal dermatitis includes frequent nappy changes, gentle cleansing, barrier protection, and topical antifungal treatment (clotrimazole 1% or miconazole 2% twice per day. A combined antifungal-mild steroid preparation may be appropriate for moderate cases. Parents should be educated about preventive measures including frequent nappy changes, nappy free time, avoiding powders or soaps, and proper cleaning techniques.
learningPoints: Nappy rash presenting with erythematous patches in skin folds with satellite lesions suggests candidal infection.
Combined antifungal and mild steroid preparations are appropriate for moderate candidal nappy dermatitis.
references: Cohen B. Differential Diagnosis of Diaper Dermatitis. Clinical Pediatrics. 2017.  Available at: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0009922817706982
Royal Children's Hospital Clinical Guidelines.  Nappy Rash. Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne. 2024.  Available at: https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/nappy_rash/
